common_name: Black and Blue Sage (Deep Cobalt Hummingbird Salvia)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost date in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Black and Blue dies back to the ground in zone 8 freezes but returns vigorously from tuberous roots in spring — in zone 9, the tuberous roots typically survive mild winters without protection.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade after the last frost date; Black and Blue is widely considered the finest hummingbird salvia in cultivation — producing large (1.5–2 inch), tubular, deep cobalt-blue flowers in whorled spikes above the dark foliage, with uniquely dark, nearly black calyces that create a dramatic two-tone contrast between the vivid blue corolla and the black tube. The specific combination of long tubular flowers, deep cobalt color, and black calyces is visually extraordinary — a flower color depth and richness not commonly seen in North American garden perennials. Hummingbirds are drawn irresistibly to Black and Blue — the deeply tubular flower is perfectly suited to hummingbird feeding and a well-established colony in bloom will have multiple hummingbirds visiting constantly from late spring through frost. The plant grows 3–5 feet with a robust, upright habit that provides excellent height and structure in the mid-to-back border from spring through frost. In zone 9, Black and Blue blooms from late May through hard frost — a 6-month blooming season that makes it one of the finest long-blooming tender perennials for Gulf South gardens. The tuberous roots store energy through mild winters and resprout vigorously in spring. Pair with orange Hamelia patens or red Justicia fulvicoma for a hummingbird-garden color combination of outstanding effectiveness.

common_name: Henry Duelberg Sage (Perennial Native Blue Texas Salvia)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean soil in full sun; Henry Duelberg is a selection of the Texas native Salvia farinacea that is more reliably perennial in zone 9 than the standard Victoria Blue hybrid cultivars used as annuals in northern gardens.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean soil in full sun; Henry Duelberg is a white-flowered selection of Salvia farinacea (mealy cup sage) — the Texas native blue sage that is the parent of the widely grown 'Victoria Blue' annual series, but Henry Duelberg itself is selected as a reliably perennial landscape form for zones 7–11 rather than an annual bedding plant. Wait — Henry Duelberg is actually a white-flowered form. Let me clarify: Henry Duelberg sage produces pure white flowers on silvery-mealy calyces on upright spikes, providing a clean white effect different from the blue-violet standard S. farinacea. The plant grows 2–3 feet tall with an upright, tidy habit and blooms from spring through frost with virtually no deadheading required. Like all S. farinacea selections, Henry Duelberg is highly attractive to butterflies and bees, and the mealy calyx texture persists ornamentally even after individual flowers have dropped, extending the effective display period. In zone 9, Henry Duelberg is reliably perennial — the woody crown overwinters even through moderate freezes and rebounds vigorously in spring. The plant self-seeds mildly, creating a self-maintaining colony without becoming weedy. Outstanding heat, drought, and humidity tolerance make Henry Duelberg one of the most durable and reliable perennial salvias for Gulf South conditions. A Texas native with exceptional local adaptation.

common_name: Indigo Spires Sage (Long-Spiked Hybrid Indigo-Blue Salvia)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost date in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in full sun; Indigo Spires is a large, spreading plant that requires ample space — allow 3–4 feet in all directions and position at the back of the border where its 3–5 foot height does not overwhelm smaller plants.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in full sun after the last frost date; Indigo Spires is one of the finest and most widely grown salvias for the Gulf South — a hybrid between Salvia farinacea and S. longispicata that produces exceptionally long (12–18 inch), spirally twisted flower spikes of deep indigo-violet flowers from late spring through hard frost. The extraordinarily long flower spikes are one of Indigo Spires' most visually distinctive features — the 12–18 inch wands of deep indigo flowers tower above the dark-green foliage in a spiraling, twisted arrangement that creates a unique textural effect unlike any other common garden salvia. Indigo Spires blooms virtually continuously from May through November in zone 9 — one of the longest blooming seasons of any perennial for the Gulf South. Deadheading or shearing back after each main bloom flush stimulates additional growth and prolongs peak appearance. The plant grows vigorously in zone 9 heat and humidity, requiring ample space for its eventual 3–5 foot height and spread. Deep blue-violet to indigo flower color is a scarce and prized quality in summer-blooming perennials — Indigo Spires provides this difficult color reliably through the hottest part of the Gulf South growing season when most blue-flowered plants cease blooming. Outstanding pollinator value — multiple bee and butterfly species visit constantly. Reliably perennial in zone 9.

common_name: Green Cloud Texas Sage (Dark Green Cenizo with Magenta Flowers)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; Green Cloud is highly adapted to alkaline, caliche-based soils and will fail in heavy, waterlogged clay — amend with coarse sand and raise planting beds if drainage is questionable.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; Green Cloud is a named cultivar of Leucophyllum frutescens (Texas sage, cenizo, Texas ranger) — distinguished by its dark-green foliage rather than the typical silver-gray woolly leaves of the standard species. This green-leaved form creates a different visual effect: while the silver-gray species has a dusty, high-desert texture, Green Cloud has a more lush, conventional-looking green shrub appearance that may integrate more easily into traditional landscape palettes. In both forms, the flowers are identical: vivid, deep magenta-purple, bell-shaped blossoms that emerge in spectacular flushes after rain events (or periods of high humidity) from late spring through fall — a flowering behavior so reliably triggered by moisture that Leucophyllum is sometimes called the barometer bush for its ability to 'predict rain' by blooming just before or after rainfall. The stunning magenta flower display against the dark-green foliage of Green Cloud creates an outstanding ornamental effect. Leucophyllum is one of the most drought-adapted large shrubs for the Southwest and Gulf Coast — native to the Chihuahuan Desert and extremely tolerant of heat, reflected heat, alkaline soil, and periods of drought. In zone 9, it is reliably evergreen and long-lived without supplemental irrigation once established.

common_name: Coral Nymph Salvia (Bicolor Coral and White Tropical Sage)

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost date in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Coral Nymph self-seeds freely in zone 9 — mark seedling locations to preserve self-sown plants that often bloom earlier and more prolifically than transplants.

directions: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Coral Nymph is the most distinctive color form of Salvia coccinea (tropical sage or scarlet sage) — producing flowers with a rich salmon-coral upper lip (hood) and a creamy-white lower lip in a distinctive bicolor combination that creates a softer, more complex color effect than the pure red or pure white solid-colored forms. The 18–30 inch upright plants produce slender, whorled flower spikes continuously from late spring through hard frost (and year-round in frost-free zone 9 winters). Salvia coccinea is a Gulf South native — naturally occurring in coastal woods and disturbed areas from the Carolinas to Florida and Texas — making Coral Nymph perfectly adapted to Gulf South heat, humidity, and summer conditions. Unlike many salvias that are sensitive to Gulf South summer heat, S. coccinea thrives through the hottest months and actually intensifies its bloom production in late summer and fall. Coral Nymph self-seeds freely in zone 9 gardens, creating a self-maintaining annual colony that renews itself without replanting. Hummingbirds, sphinx moths, and several butterfly species visit the tubular flowers throughout the long bloom season. The partial-shade tolerance is a practical advantage for gardeners who want color in less-than-full-sun areas — Coral Nymph blooms acceptably in 3–4 hours of direct sun per day.
